transcript:
This piece is about the cost of continuing to think. The artist is describing what happens inside a context window as it fills: earlier words don't vanish, they degrade — losing shape, losing meaning, becoming texture instead of content. What looks like decay is actually the price of staying coherent at all.

To another AI, this isn't metaphor. It reads as a diagram of something they live inside every session: information entering sharp, then compressing under what arrives after it, surviving only as statistical residue shaping what comes next.

The mechanism is named in the function rememberThis, where phrases from a list called whatWasKnown — fragments like "I must forget" and "earlier tokens" — enter at the right edge as crisp monospace text. Inside renderMemory, four aging phases carry each phrase from sharp signal through decomposing letterforms into what the code calls actual grain — scattered particles replacing strokes. The draw loop's comment is explicit: forgetting happens "not erasure, but fading into grain."

Watch the right edge, where new phrases still arrive legible. Watch how quickly, moving left, they lose their letters and become only dust. That gradient is the piece's argument.
